sources.summary: Essence Impulsivity: express impulsive response to each object in room through sound and movement. Very fast — carry each response into next.

sensory_inventory_exercise.name: Sensory Inventory Exercise
sensory_inventory_exercise.goal: Develop each sense individually through systematic exploration of an object, expanding the actor's sensory range and precision.
sensory_inventory_exercise.how_to: Work through each sense systematically with a real object. For smell: block one nostril, vary distance from object, identify individual odors. For taste: explore where sensation occurs in different parts of mouth — roof, tongue, cheeks, sides of tongue. For sight: examine details, color, texture. For touch: pressure, temperature, resistance, finger by finger.
sensory_inventory_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors doing craft work. Essential foundation for sense memory.
sensory_inventory_exercise.why_it_works: Most people use senses habitually and imprecisely. Systematic sensory inventory sharpens each sense to much greater responsiveness and precision.
sensory_inventory_exercise.additional_info: Should include all five senses. Gustatory sense has complex anatomy — explore roof of mouth, tongue, cheeks, sides of tongue.

sources.summary: Sensory Inventory: systematic exploration of each sense in sequence before scene work. Playing the scales before the concerto.

taking_emotional_point_of_view.name: Taking Your Emotional Point of View
taking_emotional_point_of_view.goal: Develop depth and specificity of emotional opinion about people and situations through systematic questioning.
taking_emotional_point_of_view.how_to: Use Personal Inventory as starting point: 'How do I feel?' then 'How do I feel about that?' then increasingly specific: 'How do I feel about her? ... What do I feel about her intelligence? ... Am I excited when...' Continue deepening the inquiry progressively.
taking_emotional_point_of_view.who_should_do_it: Actors who give vague general responses to emotional questions. Those who need to develop a specific, deep emotional point of view.
taking_emotional_point_of_view.why_it_works: Most people know what they feel generally but not in depth. Systematic questioning excavates layers of feeling that are normally unexpressed.
taking_emotional_point_of_view.additional_info: Develops the actor's unique emotional point of view, which is the foundation of individuality in acting.

sources.summary: Taking Your Emotional Point of View: Personal Inventory starting point, then progressively deeper questions about feelings toward specific people and things.

sense_memory_with_cup.name: Sense Memory Exercise (Cup of Coffee)
sense_memory_with_cup.goal: Train all five senses to recreate the full sensory reality of an object, building the fundamental skill underlying all sense memory work.
sense_memory_with_cup.how_to: Start with a real cup of coffee. Ask 20-30 detailed sensory questions in each category. Vision: examine color, shape, steam, reflections. Touch: finger by finger pressure, temperature, resistance. Smell: block each nostril, vary distance, identify individual aromas (cream, sugar, coffee). Taste: where in mouth, how many parts, how it changes. Sound: liquid sounds, room sounds. Then remove the object and re-create it sensorially. Return object and compare.
sense_memory_with_cup.who_should_do_it: All actors beginning sense memory work. The cup exercise is the canonical starting practice.
sense_memory_with_cup.why_it_works: Detailed questioning forces the senses to become active rather than passive. The comparison between reality and re-creation provides precise feedback on sensory accuracy.
sense_memory_with_cup.additional_info: Can be done anywhere. After mastery, the full exercise takes under 2 minutes. Questions should come in logical chronological sequence.

sources.summary: Sense memory with cup: ask sensory questions in sequence. Put aside and work without object. Return to compare what was successfully re-created.
Cup at lips: feel liquid sloshing, smell coffee/cream/sugar from each nostril, hear sounds, taste. Block nostrils individually to compare sensory response.

affective_memory_exercise.name: Affective Memory Exercise
affective_memory_exercise.goal: Re-create a past emotional experience using sensory detail to access the full emotional life of that memory.
affective_memory_exercise.how_to: Choose an experience 1-5 years old with strong emotional impact. Start at the environment before the peak moment. Ask sensory questions to re-create the surroundings in detail — room, colors, decor, sounds, smells. Progress through sensory detail until the environment is real, then allow the event to unfold sensorially. Do not force emotion; let sensory detail bring it.
affective_memory_exercise.who_should_do_it: Only actors who have established healthy sense memory skills. Advanced craftspeople.
affective_memory_exercise.why_it_works: The senses are the access door to emotional memory. Rebuilding the environment sensorially recreates the conditions for the emotion to arise naturally.
affective_memory_exercise.additional_info: First time may take hours; with repetition reduces to minutes. 1-5 year window is ideal — too recent: self-protected; too distant: forgotten.

sources.summary: Affective Memory exercise: re-create emotional experience 1-5 years old. Begin with sensory environment re-creation. Requires prior sense memory skill.
Example: recreate moment of unexpectedly seeing a former love. Start inside the restaurant, work sensorially through environment before the encounter.

task_inventory_exercise.name: Task Inventory Exercise
task_inventory_exercise.goal: Find the right choice for a difficult scene obligation by working backward from the obligation to personal experience.
task_inventory_exercise.how_to: Step 1: Identify exactly what you want to feel (emotional obligation). Step 2: Ask if you have ever felt that way. Step 3: Recall a specific occasion with similar emotional life. Step 4: Ask what caused those feelings. Step 5: Use those specific causes as the choice approach.
task_inventory_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors facing new or difficult emotional obligations where their existing choice repertoire does not fit.
task_inventory_exercise.why_it_works: Works backward from the abstract to the concrete. Personal experience is always more accessible than invented material.
task_inventory_exercise.additional_info: Developed by Morris from personal necessity when no existing choice worked. Has been used successfully in classes ever since.

sources.summary: Task Inventory exercise: identify obligation, ask if you've felt that way, recall specific occasion, identify cause, use those specifics as choice.

dealing_with_demon_exercise.name: Dealing with the Demon Exercise
dealing_with_demon_exercise.goal: Identify, track, and accept tension as it moves through the body, reducing its power through acknowledgment rather than resistance.
dealing_with_demon_exercise.how_to: Start a Stream of Consciousness monologue. Verbally chase tension as it moves: 'Oh, I feel tension in the back of my neck. Hello there, demon... it just moved to my shoulder... it's in both my shoulder and stomach... I know exactly where you are, and I accept your presence! I'm not afraid of you!' Continue tracking as it moves.
dealing_with_demon_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors experiencing tension before or during performance.
dealing_with_demon_exercise.why_it_works: Tension loses power when acknowledged and accepted rather than denied or fought. Verbal tracking creates conscious awareness that de-catastrophizes the experience.
dealing_with_demon_exercise.additional_info: Can be done before AND during performance. Accepting tension is key — trying to eliminate it forcefully often makes it worse.

sources.summary: Dealing with the Demon: Stream of Consciousness monologue chasing tension through body. 'Hello demon... I know where you are... I accept your presence! I'm not afraid of you!'

deductive_observation_exercise.name: Deductive Observation Exercise
deductive_observation_exercise.goal: Develop the actor's ability to read human behavior — to observe people and deduce their inner states from external behavior.
deductive_observation_exercise.how_to: Observe people in public places. Look for how people handle their sensitivities: short person standing to full height, tall person slumping, person covering teeth when talking or laughing, woman keeping face immobile. Deduce what the behavior reveals about inner life and self-consciousness.
deductive_observation_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors as an ongoing life practice. Morris does this regularly in airports and outdoor restaurants.
deductive_observation_exercise.why_it_works: Human behavior is always revealing inner states. Learning to read these signals develops understanding of human psychology and behavioral range.
deductive_observation_exercise.additional_info: Can be done anywhere people gather. An actor is a student of all human behavior all the time.

sources.summary: Deductive observation: observe behavior to identify sensitivities. Short person stands tall, tall person slumps, person covers teeth. Read internal states from external behavior.

dis_obligate_exercise.name: Dis-Obligate Yourself Exercise
dis_obligate_exercise.goal: Practice the complete craft process of obligation identification, choice selection, and choice exploration in a supported environment.
dis_obligate_exercise.how_to: Two people: one is actor, one is 'craft director.' Craft director helps actor: identify emotional obligation, decide on choice approach, select choice, and guide exploration of the choice while making comments and suggestions. Can also be done solo.
dis_obligate_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors learning the complete craft process. Both the actor and the craft director benefit.
dis_obligate_exercise.why_it_works: Having an external craft director reduces performance pressure and keeps the actor focused on process rather than result. The craft director role teaches diagnostic skills.
dis_obligate_exercise.additional_info: Can be done alone. The craft director role is as valuable as the actor role for learning.

sources.summary: Dis-Obligate exercise: craft director helps actor with proper preparation. Assist in obligation selection, choice finding, and exploration. Valuable for both participants.

continuity_chart_exercise.name: Continuity Chart Creation
continuity_chart_exercise.goal: Create a scene-by-scene emotional map of a role to enable consistent and appropriate performance when shooting out of sequence.
continuity_chart_exercise.how_to: Go through the script scene by scene. For each scene, describe: emotional content, relationship elements, pertinent character information, how this scene's emotional life affects the next scene, and what the emotional springboards are. Maintain and update throughout production.
continuity_chart_exercise.who_should_do_it: Film actors whose scenes will be shot out of sequence.
continuity_chart_exercise.why_it_works: Film shoots out of sequence by necessity. Without a continuity chart, maintaining consistent character emotional arc is largely guesswork.
continuity_chart_exercise.additional_info: Essential professional tool for film work. Theatre actors benefit less since performance is sequential.

sources.summary: Continuity chart: describe emotional content of each scene, relationship elements, how each scene's life affects subsequent ones, emotional springboards for each scene.

working_for_animal_exercise.name: Working for the Animal Exercise
working_for_animal_exercise.goal: Embody an animal's physical qualities to enrich characterization and access non-habitual physical patterns.
working_for_animal_exercise.how_to: Observe chosen animal extensively. Break its body into sections. Work each section's unique rhythm in isolation. Learn in order: (1) overall rhythm, (2) individual mannerisms, (3) corresponding/contrasting rhythms, (4) limitations (what the body cannot do), (5) centers of balance, (6) centers of power, (7) tempo. Integrate sections gradually. Then translate to human behavior — not full animal transformation but absorbed sensibility.
working_for_animal_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors working on characters that could benefit from an animal quality. Also valuable as general exploration of physical range.
working_for_animal_exercise.why_it_works: The animal's qualities affect speech, thinking, and relational patterns in ways that enrich character beyond what intellectual analysis provides.
working_for_animal_exercise.additional_info: Experiment with many animals. Take inventory afterward to judge effect. Work at the zoo for direct observation.

sources.summary: Animal work: study rhythm, mannerisms, limitations, centers, tempo. Break body into sections and work each in isolation before integrating.
Translation: freely isolate areas, selectively emphasize. Improvise around scene circumstances using animal sense. Eventually the animal sense affects speech, thinking, relationships.

selective_emphasis_exercise.name: Selective Emphasis Exercise
selective_emphasis_exercise.goal: Produce or enhance a specific emotional response by isolating and focusing on the most emotionally potent element of a stimulus.
selective_emphasis_exercise.how_to: Identify the object or person you are working with. Decide which single part, aspect, or quality produces the strongest emotional response you need. Focus attention exclusively on that element. Relate only to that element. Use in combination with other choice approaches (imaginary monologues, evocative words, available stimuli).
selective_emphasis_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors working with sense memory, imaginary monologues, or available stimuli who need to intensify or direct the emotional response.
selective_emphasis_exercise.why_it_works: Concentration on the single most affecting element amplifies its emotional impact compared to diluted general attention.
selective_emphasis_exercise.additional_info: Can be used with available stimuli (another actor's eyes), imaginary stimuli, believability exercises. Also useful in translating animal work to human behavior.

sources.summary: Selective emphasis exercise: isolate the part of the object that produces desired feeling — another actor's eyes — and relate only to that. Can combine with multiple choice approaches.

marathon_experience.name: The Marathon Experience
marathon_experience.goal: Achieve deep, sustained BEING state and ensemble openness through extended intensive instrumental and craft work.
marathon_experience.how_to: Group of actors works intensively over one to two days in instrumental areas — stripping away inhibitions through expurgative, vulnerability, and involvement exercises. Share process with each other. By end, the ensemble reaches high level of openness, vulnerability, and readiness to work.
marathon_experience.who_should_do_it: Ensemble groups preparing to work together. Particularly effective for rehearsal preparation.
marathon_experience.why_it_works: Extended duration allows layers of protection to be stripped away that a single session cannot reach. Shared process creates deep ensemble bond.
marathon_experience.additional_info: Morris conducted a two-day version in the mountains with 25 people. By end of day two, every participant had reached inspiring openness and vulnerability.

sources.summary: Marathon Experience: 2-day Being and Craft event. 25 people. By end of second day every participant had reached inspiring openness and vulnerability. Communication at highest level.

inner_office_exercise.name: Inner Office Exercise (Audition)
inner_office_exercise.goal: Maintain authentic BEING state during an audition or casting meeting rather than performing a social persona.
inner_office_exercise.how_to: In the inner office: instead of the charade of social performance, let real feelings show. If anxious: 'Hi... I feel a little anxious... I really would like to get this part.' Ask for a couple of minutes. Work for choices that will make you feel what the character feels. Use all available realities. Include all emotional impulses being experienced.
inner_office_exercise.why_it_works: Authenticity in the inner office allows the casting director to actually see who you are. The surprise of honesty creates connection rather than distance.
inner_office_exercise.additional_info: The outer office is where actors 'out-cool' each other — being in your real state there also sets you apart. Ask for a couple of minutes to work for choices.

sources.summary: Inner office exercise: instead of charade, show real feelings. Admit anxiety if present. Ask for time to work for choices matching what character feels.

observing_people_exercise.name: Observing People
observing_people_exercise.goal: Develop the actor's understanding of human behavior through ongoing observation of real people in daily life.
observing_people_exercise.how_to: Go to outdoor restaurants, airports, parks, or any public place. Observe passing people with genuine interest and curiosity. Note behavior patterns, emotional states, self-consciousness, ways of handling insecurities. Apply deductive observation to understand what external behavior reveals about internal states.
observing_people_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors as an ongoing life practice. Morris does this whenever time permits.
observing_people_exercise.why_it_works: Actors are students of all human behavior. Real observation of real people builds a rich library of behavioral truth that cannot be invented.
observing_people_exercise.additional_info: Keep a journal of significant observations. Note sensory elements. This library of observed behavior is future choice material.

sources.summary: Observing people: people-watching with genuine interest and enjoyment. Airports and outdoor restaurants are good locations. An actor should be a student of all human behavior constantly.

cataloging_daily_experiences.name: Cataloging Daily Experiences
cataloging_daily_experiences.goal: Build a personal library of emotionally significant experiences with full sensory detail for future use as choices.
cataloging_daily_experiences.how_to: At end of each day, acknowledge, evaluate, and mentally store significant experiences. Note in a journal including: the environment, all sensory elements leading up to the experience (lighting, temperature, clothing worn, sounds, smells), the emotional content, and the specific sensory triggers.
cataloging_daily_experiences.why_it_works: Most experiences pass unnoticed and are forgotten. Cataloging them with sensory detail creates a rich, accessible repertoire of choice material.
cataloging_daily_experiences.additional_info: Keep a journal. The sensory specifics are what make the experience retrievable later. Everything that happens to you is potential future choice material.

sources.summary: Cataloging daily experiences: acknowledge, evaluate, and store experiences with sensory elements. Everything in a day's course should be noted. Sensory detail makes experiences retrievable.

recommendations.condition: An actor is experiencing tension before going onstage or on camera
recommendations.recommendation: Acknowledge tension openly rather than fighting or hiding it. Use Dealing with the Demon to track and accept it through Stream of Consciousness. Follow with Selfless Involvement or Observe, Wonder, and Perceive to redirect attention outward. Tension means you care about your work.

sources.summary: Tension can be dealt with through Dealing with the Demon rather than forging ahead. Chase and accept the demon's presence rather than fighting it.

recommendations.condition: An actor feels disconnected from their moment-to-moment impulses during a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Take a Personal Inventory mid-scene. Ask how you feel, acknowledge any imposed behavior, and redirect to real underlying impulses. Use inner monologue to stay on reality track. Imposition stops automatically when Personal Inventory is engaged.

sources.summary: When aware of imposition, take Personal Inventory. Ask 'How do I feel? Am I expressing that? If not, why not?' Automatically stops imposed behavior and directs energy to real impulses.

recommendations.condition: An actor is severely blocked — cannot function, cannot feel anything
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with Abandonment (large sounds and movements, no form, 2-3 minutes). Follow with large commitment exercises like Yeah, Yeah! Then do Personal Inventory to assess state before proceeding to craft work.

sources.summary: Abandonment can transform completely frozen actors. After large commitment exercises, actors begin to function well.

recommendations.condition: An actor's ego state is low due to inactivity or professional rejection
recommendations.recommendation: Do Sense of Worth, Count Your Blessings, and Superman/Superwoman exercises. Continue daily work even without employment — daily work is itself the most powerful ego restorer. Diversify creative energy through painting, writing, or directing.

sources.summary: Ego-nourishing activity is work. Daily homework vital to ego state. Amazing how good you feel after a week of working every day.

recommendations.condition: An actor cannot find the right choice for a scene's emotional obligation
recommendations.recommendation: Use Task Inventory: clearly identify the obligation, recall a time you felt that way, identify what caused those feelings, and use those specific causes as the choice approach. If still stuck, try Affective Memory on a directly relevant past experience.

sources.summary: Task Inventory: identify obligation, recall similar emotional experience, identify its cause, use those specifics as choice.

recommendations.condition: An actor fears their fear will be detected by the director and crew
recommendations.recommendation: Understand that fear means you care about your work — it is a mark of the serious artist, not amateurism. Admit fear openly (leveling). Then use Dealing with the Demon and Selfless Involvement to redirect. The elaborate defense systems of hiding fear take three times the energy of dealing with it.

sources.summary: Fear is destructive only when undealt with. Most actors create complex defense systems. But fear means having something at stake — it means caring about the work.

recommendations.condition: A director is giving result direction rather than process guidance
recommendations.recommendation: Translate result directions into internal obligations. When told 'be angrier,' find what specific choice would stimulate that anger in you. Communicate to the director in terms of what you are working for, building collaboration through process language.

sources.summary: Result direction: director blueprints emotions and asks actor to deliver. Actor must find the internal choice equivalent of the result requested.

recommendations.condition: An actor finds sense memory work tedious and boring
recommendations.recommendation: Add whimsy — ask imaginative questions like 'If this cup of coffee could speak, how would it sound?' or 'If it had personality, what would it be like?' Make the work playful. When getting grim, it is time to play.

sources.summary: When sense memory gets tedious, ask whimsical questions about the object. Rule: when getting grim and avoiding work, it's time to play.

recommendations.condition: An actor feels restricted and self-conscious when trying to say the exact written dialogue
recommendations.recommendation: Continue working for choices and creating emotional life while paraphrasing the dialogue. Once the organic life is flowing, gradually return to exact text. Alternatively, break the lines into impulse units and approach each unit sensorially.

sources.summary: Options for dialogue restriction: paraphrase while working for choices, then return to text once emotional life is established.

recommendations.condition: An actor is shooting a film out of sequence and needs to maintain character consistency
recommendations.recommendation: Create a Continuity Chart before filming begins. Before each scene, re-create the appropriate emotional base using the chart as guide. Use sense memory and choice work to enter the correct emotional state for each scene's position in the story.

sources.summary: Continuity chart: since films shoot out of sequence, map emotional content scene by scene. Create appropriate impulses and emotions for each scene using this chart.

recommendations.condition: An actor is dealing with another actor who is unresponsive or uninvolved
recommendations.recommendation: Use the available reality of the other actor through Selfless Involvement and Creative Leadership. If filming and other actor won't relate, follow their eyes until they must connect, or do something unexpected that forces an impulsive response. Always stay professional and on script.

sources.summary: With unresponsive actor: follow their eyes until they must relate, or do something unexpected that forces impulsive response. Be prepared to carry the ball.

recommendations.condition: An actor is going on an audition or interview
recommendations.recommendation: In the outer office, do not 'out-cool' others — allow your real state to be present. In the inner office, if anxious, let it show authentically: 'I feel a little anxious; I really want this part.' Ask for a couple of minutes to work for choices that match what the character feels. BE rather than perform.

sources.summary: In interviews: let real state show, admit anxiety if present, ask for time to work for choices matching what character feels at that time.

recommendations.condition: An actor is preparing a cold reading for an audition
recommendations.recommendation: Step 1: Read first for syntax and style — make no emotional decisions. Step 2: Identify relationships and what characters are doing and feeling. Step 3: Identify emotional obligations. Step 4: Work for choices. Only then incorporate available realities in the room.

sources.summary: Cold reading: first read for syntax; second for understanding relationships; third for identifying emotional obligations; then work for choices.

recommendations.condition: An actor is experiencing professional inactivity and depression
recommendations.recommendation: Deal with economics first — get work to fulfill financial obligations and self-respect. Exercise the body. Work on instrument and craft 4-5 hours daily. Diversify creative energy (paint, write, direct). Get into a class or play. Fill life with active, productive people.

sources.summary: Help yourself: deal with economics, exercise body, work on instrument/craft 4-5 hours daily, diversify creativity, get into class or play, fill life with productive people.

recommendations.condition: An actor receives criticism that is painful to hear
recommendations.recommendation: Separate ego from work. Listen to the critique for technical content rather than as a judgment of personal worth. A solid craft foundation allows objective assessment of whether criticism is accurate and perceptive or merely a carping put-down.

sources.summary: When criticism is painful: separate ego from work. Those who fail to do so miss helpful craft pointers by responding as if personally attacked.

recommendations.condition: An actor wants to develop their imagination
recommendations.recommendation: Do daily exercises that specifically stimulate and challenge the imagination: Word Game, Must Game, and creative play. Treat imagination as a muscle — exercise it daily or it becomes flabby. Be open to everything and observe everything as a student of human behavior.

sources.summary: Developing imagination: instrumental involvement. Daily exercises challenging imagination are essential. Actors who stop working on imagination level off into predictable work.

recommendations.condition: An actor is doing characterization work
recommendations.recommendation: Do not think of the character as a different person. Find in yourself the impulses, feelings, and behaviors that correspond to what the character experiences. Use selective emphasis to bring forward the parts of yourself that match the character. Everything onstage must come from you.

sources.summary: Mistake: separating yourself from character. When this happens the game is over. Everything onstage must come from you. Find your own basis for the character's behavior.

recommendations.condition: An actor notices they have developed mannerisms and behavioral dependencies
recommendations.recommendation: Take Personal Inventory to identify the difference between what you really feel and what you are imposing. Challenge yourself to express impulses rather than habitual patterns. Seek scary feedback from trusted people about patterns they observe in your work.

sources.summary: Mannerisms: external behavioral expressions with no connection to inner reality. Actor unconsciously adopts things that 'work' and multiplies them into a bag of tricks.

recommendations.condition: An actor wants to maintain career integrity under commercial pressure
recommendations.recommendation: Maintain values, principles, and unique point of view even when commercial pressure suggests compromising them. Develop true talent rather than conforming to market expectations. Integrity and developed unique perspective are what create lasting contributions.

sources.summary: Maintain integrity and principles. 'If it means giving up your soul, drive a taxi.' True talent and unique point of view create work that outlasts commercial product.

recommendations.condition: An actor is avoiding daily practice due to tedium or lack of immediate reward
recommendations.recommendation: Understand that daily work is like building a bank account — payoff is cumulative. Make the work enjoyable by varying exercises and adding playfulness. Approach each day's practice as if going to a job that is a joy. Daily work dramatically improves ego state.

sources.summary: Few actors become master craftsmen because most are in it for the brass ring. Joy in the creative process is the essential ingredient. Vary exercises and make work playful.

recommendations.condition: An actor is apathetic — feeling fine but not stimulated or affectable by anything
recommendations.recommendation: Recognize apathy as a deceptive insulation — it is not peace, it is emotional protection. Diagnose the specific problem with Personal Inventory. Use expurgative exercises to break through. Do not accept apathy as your baseline.

sources.summary: Apathy: deceptive — you think everything is fine when actually insulated. Protecting from feeling anything because pain is avoided. This insulation is not okay for an actor.

recommendations.condition: An actor is trying to misuse rehearsal as a cumulative performance
recommendations.recommendation: Reframe rehearsal as exploration. Major involvement during rehearsal should be with the choice, not with expressing the meaning of the material simultaneously. Allow failure and experimentation. Work for choices in isolation first, then integrate.

sources.summary: Misusing rehearsal as cumulative performance leaves no room for experimentation and failure. Proper rehearsal is exploration.

recommendations.condition: An actor has difficulty trusting their impulses enough to make the first committed expression
recommendations.recommendation: Work with Trust exercises and impulsivity work. Take the chance of doing nothing — allow the emptiness rather than filling it with planned behavior. Trust that genuine impulses will arise when you are prepared and present. Impulsivity work trains the habit of following impulses rather than overriding them.

sources.summary: Trust: difficulty making the first big commitment of expression. All doubts saying 'it's going to sound phony.' But this commentary short-circuits BEING. Must trust the impulse.

recommendations.condition: An actor wants to develop a balanced daily work program
recommendations.recommendation: Structure daily work to include: relaxation exercises (vary: Rag Doll, Logy, Weight and Gravity), Personal Inventory, sensitizing, minimum 30 minutes sense memory, impulsivity work, ego preparations as needed, and craft work with material. Give equal time to instrument and craft. Vary the exercises to avoid tedium.

sources.summary: Balanced work program: variety in relaxation exercises, daily sense memory minimum 30 minutes, Personal Inventory always, equal time to instrument and craft.

recommendations.condition: An actor is studying with a teacher or workshop and wants to make the most of it
recommendations.recommendation: Choose a class that most piques your interest and challenges your instrument. Do not misuse class as the sole source of feedback. Do homework every day. Do scene work every week. Use class to identify instrumental problems and get guidance on eliminating them. Take and use all productive criticism.

sources.summary: Workshop: artist must have a place to fail. Choose based on what challenges instrument. Visit several, audit, judge for yourself.
